comment.body: Do you think as we move up the economic/power scale from retail to private equity to hedge funds to governments, doesn’t that scale tip from “perception” to “reality”? My point is, there will need to be some convincing of “reality” before the biggest players respond and capitulate in the way that Alma said is needed. I think that’s what Jerome Powell hinted to and why it might take a while for him to react, along with other players.

comment.body: A lot of that information takes time to wander through the system. Bigger funds have investment committees that decide about major reductions in exposure. They need to set up meetings, prepare slides, discuss, argue, make a decision... And then there might be people who have the opposite opinion as you and see this as a great buying opportunity... Try to stay neutral about your assessment of the market moves. You will burn much less mental capital with that. Not every move has a reason other than a short term imbalance between buyers and sellers
comment.raw_body: A lot of that information takes time to wander through the system. Bigger funds have investment committees that decide about major reductions in exposure. comment.body:
Yes, but at what point will the perception start moving what some of these large players are doing?  If they perceive a 5% chance of all hell breaking loose, how much risk to they take off the table?  If in two weeks we are still not near resolution on tariffs and they perceive a 10% risk, what then?  

So I definitely hear your point about them needing to see reality before they respond in a huge way.  I just think the negative perception and uncertainty are going to front-run that a bit.

I’m sorry if a sounded glib before.  Work is crazy today.

comment.body: I think you need to differentiate between the perception of what the markets does next and the perception of where the world is going. The longer this tariff saga goes on, the more important the second question becomes. If everybody is convinced this is just a short term sell off and Trump will take the tariffs off soon, we might go back to business as usual (in the perception of some investors). But the longer the situation is unclear, the more it depends on the assessment of how much damage Trumps strategy will do to the US economy long term. You can rest assured there is a whole armada of investment analysts scheming different scenarios right now. And these models will now get updated daily. And in the mean time risk will be reduced anyhow, the question is just by how much

comment.body:
Seems to me that a majority of people believe this is short term, as Alma probably does. However, it seems the true capitulation, as Alma also alluded to, will occur when everyone is convinced that this is a longer term problem that will send the US economy into a deflationary tail spin.

What data will be needed to confirm that? And how long until there is a visible trend in the data?

So 1.5 months seems to be a tall order to convince everyone else of the latter. We will only be through one quarter of earnings and have 1 month of employment data.

But maybe 1.5 months of this uncertainty and stress will be enough to bring everyone to their knees, along with the EU throwing a curve ball with their retaliations. Crazy environment.
